items.title: Risk factors for cholelithiasis
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors for cholelithiasis

  • Female, obesity, multiparity and >40 years old (4 F rule: Female, Fat, Fertile, Forty).
  • Rapid loss of weight, total parenteral nutrition, cirrhosis and chronic hemolysis (e.g. hereditary spherocytosis) predispose to bilirubin stones.
  • Drugs: ceftriaxone (precipitated with calcium), fibrates, hormonal therapy (oral contraceptives).
  • Diabetes mellitus and dyslipidemia increase the risk of bile sludge and cholesterol stones.

items.title: Biliary colic clinic
items.contentHtml:
Clinic of biliary colic

  • Pain right hypochondrium or epigastrium, sudden onset, progressive intensity, duration 30 minutes to 6 hours.
  • Radiated to interscapular region or right shoulder (irritation phrenic).
  • Triggered by fatty foods; It is accompanied by nausea and vomiting.
  • There is no fever nor Murphy's sign (different from cholecystitis); The pain subsides spontaneously or with NSAIDs.
  • Trap examination: If the pain lasts >6 hours, suspect acute cholecystitis.

items.title: Murphy's sign in cholecystitis
items.contentHtml:
Murphy sign in cholecystitis

  • Murphy sign: interruption of deep inspiration when palpating right hypochondrium during palpation.
  • Corresponds to inflammation of the visceral peritoneum over the gallbladder; highly specific for acute cholecystitis.
  • On ultrasound: ultrasound Murphy's sign (pain when pressing with the transducer on the gallbladder) has ~90% sensitivity.
  • Trap examination: the Murphy sign may be absent in the elderly, diabetics or patients with calculous cholecystitis.

items.foco: Ultrasound as a diagnostic gold standard
items.title: Ultrasound as a diagnostic gold standard
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound as a diagnostic gold standard

  • Abdominal ultrasound is the gold standard for cholelithiasis and acute cholecystitis (sensitivity >95%).
  • Key findings in cholelithiasis: hyperechoic images with posterior acoustic shadowing that are mobilized with changes in position.
  • Findings in cholecystitis: wall thickening >4 mm, biliary sludge, perivesicular fluid and ultrasound Murphy's sign.
  • Double wall sign (subserous edema) is specific for cholecystitis acute.
  • Trap: ultrasound is not useful for choledocholithiasis (sensitivity 25-60%); prefer cholangioresonance or ERCP.

items.title: Complications: choledocholithiasis and cholangitis
items.contentHtml:
Complications: choledocholithiasis and cholangitis

  • Choledocholithiasis: lithiasis in the common bile duct; symptoms of obstructive jaundice (elevated direct bilirubin), choluria and acolia.
  • Cholangitis: bile duct infection; Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, HD pain) and Reynolds pentad adds hypotension and confusion (severe cholangitis).
  • Diagnosis: ultrasound (bile duct dilation >6 mm) + laboratory (leukocytosis, elevated alkaline phosphatase).
  • Treatment: urgent ERCP + antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone+metronidazole).

items.title: Tokyo criteria for cholecystitis
items.contentHtml:
Tokyo Criteria for Cholecystitis

  • Tokyo Criteria 2018 (TG18) classify severity of acute cholecystitis into Grade I (mild), Grade II (moderate) and Grade III (severe).
  • Major criteria: sign Murphy's syndrome, palpable mass, fever >38°C, CRP >3 mg/dL, WBC >10,000/mm³ and ultrasound findings (thickening, fluid, ultrasound Murphy).
  • Grade III (severe): organic dysfunction (hypotension, alteration consciousness, PaO2/FiO2 <300, creatinine >2 mg/dL, INR >1.5).
  • Grade II (moderate): leukocytosis >18,000, painful mass, >72 hours of evolution, perivesicular abscess.
  • Grade I (mild): no criteria of II or III.

items.title: Medical vs surgical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Medical vs surgical treatment

  • Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the definitive treatment for acute cholecystitis and symptomatic cholelithiasis.
  • Medical treatment (conservative): fasting, IV hydration, NSAIDs (ketorolac 30 mg IV) or paracetamol; antibiotics if infection is suspected (ceftriaxone 2 g/day + metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours). porcelain gallbladder, transplant, hemolytic anemia).

items.title: Indications for laparoscopic cholecystectomy
items.contentHtml:
Indications for laparoscopic cholecystectomy

  • Acute cholecystitis (ideally within 72 hours of the onset of symptoms).
  • Symptomatic cholelithiasis (recurrent biliary colic).
  • Complications of cholelithiasis: biliary pancreatitis, choledocholithiasis (after ERCP), cholecystoenteric fistula.
  • Porcelain gallbladder (risk of cancer), gallbladder polyps >10 mm, congenital anomalies (e.g. symptomatic Phrygian cap gallbladder).
  • Relative contraindication: Child-Pugh C cirrhosis, uncorrected coagulopathy, pregnancy (prefer second trimester if urgent).

items.title: Management of choledocholithiasis: ERCP
items.contentHtml:
Management of choledocholithiasis: ERCP

  • ERCP (endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography) is the treatment of choice for choledocholithiasis before or after cholecystectomy.
  • Indications: obstructive jaundice, cholangitis, bile duct dilation (>6 mm on ultrasound), elevated direct bilirubin or alkaline phosphatase.
  • Procedure: sphinterotomy + extraction with balloon or basket; if large stones (>1.5 cm), use mechanical lithotripsy.
  • Complications: post-ERCP pancreatitis (3-5%), hemorrhage, perforation, cholangitis.
  • Alternative: laparoscopic choledocholithotomy in centers with experience.

items.title: Mirizzi syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Mirizzi syndrome

  • Mirizzi syndrome: obstruction of the common hepatic duct by a stone impacted in the infundibulum or cystic duct, with inflammation and extrinsic compression.
  • Clinic: intermittent obstructive jaundice, HD pain, may simulate cholangiocarcinoma (difficult differential diagnosis).
  • Classification of Csendes: Type I (compression without fistula), Type II (cholecystocholedochal fistula, loss of <30% of circumference), Type III (>30%), Type IV (total destruction of the common bile duct).
  • Diagnosis: ERCP or cholangioresonance; Ultrasound can show a “double duct” sign.
  • Treatment: subtotal cholecystectomy + bile duct repair; Avoid dissection of Calot's triangle due to risk of injury.

items.title: Differential diagnosis with pancreatitis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with pancreatitis

  • Acute pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to the back, persistent vomiting, ileus; amylase/lipase >3 times the upper limit.
  • Cholecystitis: HD pain radiating to the right shoulder, Murphy sign, fever; ultrasound with parietal thickening.
  • Both can coexist (biliary pancreatitis) due to migrating stones; then elevated amylase + bile duct dilation.
  • Trap examination: in pancreatitis the ultrasound may be normal or show an enlarged pancreas; CT with contrast is the gold standard for pancreatitis.
  • Additional DDx: perforated peptic ulcer (pneumoperitoneum), subhepatic appendicitis (high McBurney), inferior AMI (ECG, troponins).

items.title: Postcholecystectomy complications
items.contentHtml:
Postcholecystectomy complications

  • Bile duct injury: the most serious, occurs in 0.1-0.5% of laparoscopic procedures; Strasberg classification (A to E).
  • Bile leak: due to cystic stump or Luschka duct; presents with pain, fever and biloma; management with ERCP + sphincterotomy.
  • Postcholecystectomy syndrome: recurrent pain (20-40%); causes: residual choledocholithiasis, papillary stenosis, dysfunction of the sphincter of Oddi.
  • Postcholecystectomy diarrhea: due to malabsorption of bile salts; treatment with cholestyramine.
  • Incisional hernia at trocar site (umbilical, epigastric).
